I did just want to let you know that I reached out to John to help me out with these because John is our analytics guy. He knows all about that. I am not well versed in analytics and the ad side of things so I wanted to reach out to him to make sure I was giving you a good answer. So this answer is going to be from the two of us as I answer it. So you have Madam So as your business name and the question is, so I am running ads for Madam So, a sewing product e-commerce store based in the US and my ad analytics and Google Analytics are not matching up at all. I want advice on whether to keep running them or not. So answer from me, from my knowledge of ads and analytics and when I'm in Google Analytics and looking at the analytics from our page post engagement ads is that these are never going to match up. They typically don't and that's very common that they don't match up because what happens is Facebook wants the credit and Google Analytics wants the credit. They're competing for that attribution number. So they're not very consistently going to match up at all and that's not something to be crazy concerned about. But for John's answer and this is a more in depth one, he says, as an analytics never match up perfectly. He's actually never seen a 100% agreement between the different Mar-Tech platforms. What he recommends is getting a feel for how the discrepancy is, how big the discrepancy is, and whether or not the mismatch is consistent. You can use this to estimate what performance is elsewhere. Also keep in mind that these platforms have different attribution models depending on how your ads are set up. So Facebook has options for seven-day click, one-day click, and one-day view attribution. And while Google Analytics has, by default, shows attribution to the most recent

## [03:00 - 06:00]

traffic source. So I definitely wouldn't decide to stop running ads just because the data didn't agree. You also need to weigh what the purpose of the campaign is. If it's content amplification, if you're just trying to get more of your content out there, the data mismatch isn't really important. You're just trying to get more views on your piece of content. But if it sales, what John says he's more interested in is the disconnect between your storefront results and the ad platforms results and see if there's a consistency there. Now with all that said, you should audit your tracking to make sure it's set up correctly. That would be kind of step one, start at the beginning. Make sure Google Analytics and your Facebook pixel both each fire once per page on every single page. And make sure your e-commerce integration is set up correctly with Google Analytics so we know that it is feeding the data in properly. So that's John's answer, much more in-depth answer. But to round that all out, definitely make sure your analytics are set up correctly. Just kind of go back, make sure the pixel is firing once and your Google Analytics is only firing once per page on your site and check for any discrepancies and see if they're consistent between the two.
